title: Anisotropic Surface Spin Waves as Signature of A-type Altermagnets
authors: Zhoujian Sun, Yiyuan Chen, Tao Yu, Hai-Zhou Lu, X. C. Xie
abstract: Altermagnets have attracted intense interest because they have the advantages of both ferromagnets and antiferromagnets. However, their experimental identification remains challenging, in particular for the A-type altermagnets that account for a large group of material candidates. Here, we discover a kind of anisotropic surface spin waves in A-type altermagnets, which is absent in ferromagnets and conventional antiferromagnets. The anisotropic surface spin waves arise directly from the nature of altermagnets, i.e., the spin-opposite sublattices cannot be related by translation or inversion, which breaks the combined spatial-inversion and time-reversal symmetry, leading to the anisotropic surface spin waves with two properties, the chirality-dependent top-bottom positions and chiral split constant frequency contours. We further show that these two properties can be measured experimentally from the stray field and by resonance absorption spectrum, respectively. Our results provide a signature for detecting altermagnets and will inspire spin-based logic and information-storage devices.
summary: The paper identifies a unique signature of A-type altermagnets through the discovery of anisotropic surface spin waves. These waves exhibit properties like chirality-dependent surface localization and frequency splitting that are absent in conventional magnets. These features can be experimentally detected using NV center magnetometry and resonance absorption spectroscopy.
main_problem: Experimental identification of A-type altermagnets is difficult because they lack an anomalous Hall signal, necessitating a new unique signature for detection.
main_result: The discovery of anisotropic surface spin waves that exhibit chirality-dependent top-bottom localization and frequency splitting, providing a definitive signature for A-type altermagnetism.
method: Linearization of the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equation around a collinear ground state and solving the resulting tridiagonal dynamical matrix eigenvalue problem.
model_or_system: An N-layer square lattice of A-type altermagnets with intralayer ferromagnetic and interlayer antiferromagnetic exchange, characterized by broken PT symmetry due to unequal intralayer couplings (J1 != J2).
key_observables: Chirality-dependent surface localization, chiral split constant frequency contours, magnetic stray fields, and resonance absorption spectra.
important_parameters: Broken PT symmetry condition (J1 != J2), intralayer exchange (J1, J2), interlayer exchange (J3), and easy-axis anisotropy (K).
main_assumptions: Linearization of the LLG equation, assumption of plane-wave excitations in the x-y plane, and neglect of damping for the resonance absorption calculation.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows PT symmetry breaking and anisotropic contours; Fig 2 displays spin wave dispersions, amplitude profiles, and elliptical frequency contours; Fig 3 illustrates the NV center setup and compares stray field magnitudes and scaling with the J1/J2 ratio.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of A-type altermagnet identification, presents a theoretical model of anisotropic surface spin waves, details the symmetry-breaking mechanism, proposes experimental detection methods via NV centers and resonance spectroscopy, and discusses the scaling of these signatures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Dispersion of the spin waves in the A-type altermagnet as functions of (a) the x-direction wave vector qx and (b) y-direction wave vector qy. ω is the frequency of the spin waves in units of the ferromagnetic coupling J2. The gray curves are the spin waves from the bulk. The red and blue curves are two surface spin waves with the right-handed and left-handed chirality, respectively. The right panels of (a) and (b) show the amplitudes of the surface spin waves at the energy marked by the dashed line as functions of the layer number (1 for bottom surface and 50 for top surface). (c) Equal-frequency contours in the (qx, qy) plane at ω/J2 = 8.0 (dashed lines in (a) and (b)), showing the...

FIG. 3. (a) Schematic of the experimental setup for measur- ing the stray field of the anisotropic surface spin wave using the nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center. (b) Stray field Bsw of the surface spin waves in the altermagnet as a function of the wave vector q, q = qx (q = qy) for the wave propagating along the x (y) direction at q = (qx, 0) (q = (0, qy)). ∆Bmax is the difference between the maximum stray fields of surface spin waves propagating along the x and y directions. (c) ∆Bmax as a function of the ratio of two intralayer exchange couplings J1/J2. (d) The same as (b) but for antiferromagnet. FIG. 4. (a) Schematic of the resonance absorption spec- troscopy for measuring spin waves. Microwaves at a given wave vector q can be excited by the excitation antenna and received by the detection antenna. A resonance absorption peak shows up at ω on the imaginary part of the total suscep- tibility Im[χ], if (ω, q) matches the spin wave in the magnet. (b) Left-handed circularly polarized absorption spectra for the altermagnet, given by Im[χ] as a function of the frequency ω/2π. (c) Frequency difference ∆ω between two left-handed surface spin wave resonance absorption peaks as a function of the ratio of the intralayer exchange couplings J1/J2.
